🟡 Carolinian Canada

Case Study
Y
Tags
33314415-10e1-8102-979c-d784e7b26d42, 33314415-10e1-81a5-a782-e6cef8a24b7b, 33314415-10e1-8125-9b3a-d2370358bb34, 33714415-10e1-80f0-a6d2-dfce8ad76f4b, 33314415-10e1-8139-bf23-e5d54ba36cbe
Activities
33314415-10e1-8118-8c66-f59f674e5ae7, 33314415-10e1-813e-8c8b-d0a3943fccc7, 33314415-10e1-81d8-95c2-f2167bb7e71a
background_section
## Background The Carolinian Zone (Lake Erie Lowland) stretches Windsor–Toronto—Canada’s southernmost ecoregion, at the northern edge of the eastern deciduous forest. Between the Great Lakes, its mild climate supports exceptional diversity: **~2,200** herbaceous plants, **~400** bird species, and some of the country’s most productive farmland. It is the traditional territory of Anishinaabe, Haudenosaunee, Lunaapeew, Wendat, and Attawandaron nations, many of whom remain present with long-standing treaty relationships. Pressures are acute: the zone covers only [about 0.25% of Canada’s land](https://caroliniancanada.ca/dzcib/report) but roughly **25% of its population**. About [85% of the ecoregion](https://caroliniancanada.ca/dzcib/report) is now cropland, pasture, or urban; forest cover has fallen from ~80% to ~11% and wetlands from ~28% to ~5%. The zone holds **more than half** of Canada’s federally listed species at risk—Ontario’s most imperiled region—while still supporting millions of people and high-yield agriculture. Action must fit that multi-use, multi-claim landscape. ### Origins and Network Formed in **1984** by scientists and local groups alarmed by habitat loss, Carolinian Canada advances collaborative conservation across the zone. The **2000 “Big Picture”** partnership with Ontario’s Natural Heritage Information Centre shifted planning from scattered “islands of green” toward a connected **natural heritage system**—still central to the Healthy Landscape Strategy and used by governments, conservation authorities, landowners, and communities. The coalition names its settler-conservation roots and, since a **2018** board directive, is **Indigenizing** the network—Two-Eyed Seeing, ethical space, and expanded Indigenous leadership in land and water stewardship. ### Philosophy & Approach Carolinian Canada integrates Indigenous and Western knowledge systems in what they call a "Two-Eyed Seeing" approach. Rather than simply consulting Indigenous communities (as many conservation projects do), their projects are co-developed from the ground up in "ethical space" where Indigenous and Western worldviews are given equal weight. This is transformative because: - It acknowledges Indigenous rights, sovereignty, and contributions to land stewardship - It operates in "ethical space" where neither knowledge system dominates - It incorporates traditional ecological knowledge into both implementation and evaluation - It goes beyond consultation to active engagement, acknowledging Indigenous rights and land sovereignty, Indigenous contributions to land stewardship and conservation, and the cultural and relational significance of the land

place
Carolinian Zone, Ontario, Canada

description
Carolinian Canada is a cross-sector network working to regenerate the unique ecosystems of the Carolinian Zone in southern Ontario, from Toronto to Windsor. Grounded in the principles of reconciliation, the organization connects science, community, and business to support healthy landscapes, biodiversity, and climate-resilient communities.
vision_legacy_section
## Vision and Legacy Carolinian Canada’s long-term aim is **community-led, climate-resilient, healthy landscapes** across southern Ontario—in **reconciliation**, not only in technical conservation. The **DZCIB** is one prototype for blended, outcome-based finance, but the network’s wider role is to convene Indigenous, civic, land, and finance partners; make biodiversity easier to fund; and embed **Two-Eyed Seeing** in practice. Scaling will adapt metrics to place while keeping holistic intent. Where finance appears, it is framed as **repairing relationships**—between peoples and with land—not only transacting for acres.
website
https://caroliniancanada.ca/
bioregional_context
The Carolinian Zone is the warmest and most biodiverse region of Canada, stretching across the southernmost parts of Ontario from Toronto to Windsor. This area represents less than 1% of Canada's landmass but is home to over 40% of its native plant species and a high concentration of rare and endangered wildlife. Its temperate climate, fertile soils, and proximity to the Great Lakes have made it a center for agriculture, urban development, and transportation—putting immense pressure on its ecosystems.
category
Bioregional Organizer
